topic: Philosophy of Religion: Ancient Philosophical Influences (Plato, Aristotle)
subject: religious-studies

items.q: Is Aristotle's Prime Mover the same as the Christian creator God?
items.correct: No - the Prime Mover does not create from nothing, does not know or love the world, and moves it only as a final cause; later thinkers like Aquinas adapted rather than copied the idea.
items.misconception: Aristotle's Prime Mover is simply the God of the Bible under another name.
items.random_wrong: The Prime Mover is the engine Aristotle built to model the heavens.
items.why: The differences between the Prime Mover and classical theism are a standard evaluation point that assimilation erases.

items.q: Did Plato think the physical world is evil and worthless?
items.correct: No - it is less real than the Forms and cannot give true knowledge, but it participates in the Forms and is ordered toward the Good; Plato is not a Gnostic despiser of matter.
items.misconception: Plato taught that the material world is wicked and to be hated.
items.random_wrong: Plato believed the physical world is the only reality.
items.why: Exaggerating Plato's dualism into world-hatred distorts the theory of Forms students must evaluate.

items.q: Are Plato's Forms just concepts inside human minds?
items.correct: No - Plato held the Forms are mind-independent realities; our concepts are attempts to grasp them, and particulars merely participate in them.
items.misconception: The Forms are simply ideas that humans invent and share.
items.random_wrong: The Forms are physical objects stored in a temple in Athens.
items.why: Psychologising the Forms removes the metaphysical claim that both Aristotle's criticism and later theology engage with.

items.q: In the Analogy of the Cave, what do the shadows represent?
items.correct: The world of appearances that people mistake for reality
items.why: Correct answer: The world of appearances that people mistake for reality. “The Forms themselves” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.correct: The purpose or telos for which a thing exists
items.misconception: The matter a thing is made from
items.random_wrong: The agent that brought it about
items.why: The answer examiners want is: The purpose or telos for which a thing exists. “The matter a thing is made from” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How does Aristotle's Prime Mover move the universe?
items.correct: As a final cause, attracting motion while itself remaining unchanged
items.random_wrong: By creating the world from nothing at a moment in time
items.why: Correct answer: As a final cause, attracting motion while itself remaining unchanged. “By physically pushing the planets” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: For Plato, where does true knowledge come from?
items.why: Correct answer: Reason grasping the eternal Forms. “The five senses alone” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
